request.question: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?

title: Björk
Where Is the Line
Dancer in the Dark
List of songs recorded by Björk
I've Seen It All
Jófríður Ákadóttir
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir
Innocence (Björk song)
Michael Flessas
Selmasongs
sentences: Björk Guðmundsdóttir (] , born 21 November 1965), known as Björk ( ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.

Over her four-decade career, she has developed an eclectic musical style that draws on a wide range of influences and genres spanning electronic, pop, experimental, classical, trip hop, IDM, and avant-garde styles.
She initially became known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band The Sugarcubes, whose 1987 single "Birthday" was a hit on US and UK indie stations and a favorite among music critics.

Björk embarked on a solo career in 1993, coming to prominence as a solo artist with albums such as "Debut" (1993), "Post" (1995), and "Homogenic" (1997), while collaborating with a range of artists and exploring a variety of multimedia projects.
"Where Is the Line" is a song recorded by Icelandic singer Björk for her sixth studio album "Medúlla".
It was written by the singer herself and co-produced with longtime collaborator Mark Bell.

The track was originally intended to be released as the album's third commercial single, but its release was cancelled just before Björk released the "Drawing Restraint 9" soundtrack, in order to focus all promotional efforts on the film and soundtrack instead.
Some promotional white labels vinyls were released around 2005.
A music video for the song was made by Icelandic Artist Gabríela Friðriksdóttir.
Upon the cancellation of the single, the video was used as part of a video installation project by Friðriksdóttir.
Dancer in the Dark (Danish: Danser i mørket) is a 2000 Danish musical drama film directed by Lars von Trier.

It stars Icelandic singer Björk as a daydreaming immigrant factory worker who suffers from a degenerative eye condition and is saving up to pay for an operation to prevent her young son from suffering the same fate.
Catherine Deneuve, David Morse, Cara Seymour, Peter Stormare, Siobhan Fallon Hogan, and Joel Grey also star.
Icelandic singer and songwriter Björk has recorded more than one hundred songs for eight studio albums, two soundtrack albums, a compilation album, five remix albums and two collaboration albums.
She is the sole writer and producer of most of the songs included in her albums.
She also sometimes plays instruments during her recording sessions.
Moreover, she has provided credited and uncredited contributions on songs recorded by other artists, including background vocals, songwriting, remixing and production.
"I've Seen It All" is a song by Icelandic singer Björk, with lyrics by Sjón and Lars von Trier.
It was released as the first promotional single from the "Dancer in the Dark" soundtrack, "".
The song features vocals from Radiohead frontman Thom Yorke.
It was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Lyrically, it speaks of one coming to terms with the fact that they are going blind.
Jófríður Ákadóttir, known professionally as JFDR, is an Icelandic singer, songwriter and multi-instrumentalist born in 1994.
She is known for her solo project JFDR as well as her work with Samaris, Pascal Pinon and Gangly and works closely with producer and multi-instrumentalist .
Her father is Icelandic composer and musician Áki Ásgeirsson.
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir (] ; born 11 November 1986), known outside Iceland simply as Greta Salóme (] ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, and violinist in the Icelandic Symphony Orchestra.
She represented Iceland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2012 in Baku, Azerbaijan, in a duet with singer Jónsi with the song "Never Forget".
They qualified from the first semi-final into the Eurovision final and eventually finished in 20th place with 46 points.
She represented Iceland again, this time as a soloist, in the Eurovision Song Contest 2016 with the song "Hear Them Calling", where she failed to qualify.
"Innocence" is a song written and recorded by Icelandic singer Björk.
The song was released as the second single from her seventh full-length studio album, "Volta".
It is co-produced by record producer Timbaland and his protégé Danja.
The song was premiered on Björk's MySpace profile on March 19, 2007 as part of the fan contest to submit ideas for the music video.
The single was released exclusively as a digital download on July 23, 2007.
Michael C. Flessas (born June 2, 1959 in Miami, Florida), is the birth name of American actor Michael Flessas, who is of Greek ancestry.
Flessas' most notable film role was "Angry Man" in the Cannes Film Festival 2000 Palme d'Or winning film "Dancer in the Dark" directed by Danish film director Lars von Trier.
Originally, the director himself considered playing the role but, instead, the role was given to Flessas.
"Dancer in the Dark" starred Icelandic singer/actress Björk who won the Best Actress award at Cannes for her role.

French film icon, César Award winner, and Academy Award nominee Catherine Deneuve, and other noteworthy artists such as Academy Award and Tony Award winner Joel Grey, Peter Stormare, David Morse, and Stellan Skarsgård also performed in the multiple prize winning film.
One of Björk's songs for the film received an Academy Award nomination for Best Song.
Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.
It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".
In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.
The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?
hop1_docs: Mary J. Blige | Mary Jane Blige ( ; born January 11, 1971) is an American singer, songwriter, rapper, model, record producer and actress.  Starting her career as a backing singer on Uptown Records in 1989, Blige released her first album, "What's the 411? ", in 1992, and has released 13 studio albums since and made over 150 guest appearances on other albums and soundtracks.

Selmasongs | Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.  It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".  In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.  The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure | Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure is a soundtrack album, released on June 23, 2004, for the anime series "Samurai Champloo".  It is the second of four soundtrack albums released for the show.  The album was produced by Japanese DJ/producer Nujabes, and American MC/producer Fat Jon, and featured Japanese rapper Shing02 and Japanese singer-songwriter MINMI on the album's first and last tracks, respectively.
List of awards and nominations received by Anahí | Anahí is a Mexican singer-songwriter, actress, record producer and dancer.

Vespertine | Vespertine is the fourth solo album by Icelandic musician Björk, released on 27 August 2001, on One Little Indian Records.  It was recorded at locations in Spain, Iceland, and the United States in 2000; production began during the filming of "Dancer in the Dark", which was characterized by conflict between the singer and director Lars von Trier. " Björk", a self-titled coffee table book containing photographs of the singer throughout her career, was released simultaneously with the album.  "Vespertine"' s sound reflected Björk's newly found interest in the music of artists such as Opiate and Console, who were also enlisted as producers.
Charlotte Devaney | Charlotte Devaney is a British DJ, producer, singer, dancer and actress.

Howard Donald | Howard Paul Donald (born 28 April 1968), is an English singer, songwriter, drummer, pianist, dancer, DJ and record producer.  He is a member of English pop-group Take That and a judge on the German reality talent show "Got to Dance".
hop1_titles: List of awards and nominations received by Anahí
hop2_docs: Björk Guðmundsdóttir & tríó Guðmundar Ingólfssonar | Björk Guðmundsdóttir & tríó Guðmundar Ingólfssonar
Björk (book) | Björk or Björk as a book is a 192-page coffee table book published by the Icelandic singer and composer Björk Guðmundsdóttir (2001), designed by M/M Paris.

Björk (album) | Björk is the first official release (considered juvenilia) from the Icelandic singer Björk Guðmundsdóttir.  In 1976, Björk appeared on Icelandic radio singing "I Love to Love" through the music school she attended, which led her to a record deal and the release, with the help of stepfather Sævar, of her first solo album in 1977.

Gling-Gló | Gling-Gló is the first and only studio album by Björk Guðmundsdóttir & tríó Guðmundar Ingólfssonar, consisting of Björk Guðmundsdóttir on vocals, Guðmundur Ingólfsson on piano, Guðmundur Steingrímsson on drums, and Þórður Högnason on bass.  "Gling-gló" is an Icelandic onomatopoeia whose English equivalent is "ding dong", or the sound that a bell makes.

Guðmundur Gunnarsson | Guðmundur Gunnarsson (born 29 October 1945) is an Icelandic electrician and union leader.  The father of the singer Björk (Björk Guðmundsdóttir), he is a nationally known figure in his own right as the leader of the Icelandic Electricians' Union, Rafiðnaðarsamband Íslands (RSÍ).

Björk | Björk Guðmundsdóttir (] , born 21 November 1965), known as Björk ( ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.  Over her four-decade career, she has developed an eclectic musical style that draws on a wide range of influences and genres spanning electronic, pop, experimental, classical, trip hop, IDM, and avant-garde styles.  She initially became known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band The Sugarcubes, whose 1987 single "Birthday" was a hit on US and UK indie stations and a favorite among music critics.  Björk embarked on a solo career in 1993, coming to prominence as a solo artist with albums such as "Debut" (1993), "Post" (1995), and "Homogenic" (1997), while collaborating with a range of artists and exploring a variety of multimedia projects.

The Elgar Sisters | The Elgar Sisters was an Icelandic duo formed by singer Björk Guðmundsdóttir and guitarist Guðlaugur Kristinn Óttarsson in 1984.  The Elgar Sisters coexisted with KUKL, another group they were part of.
response.summary_2: The soundtrack to Dancer in the Dark is by Björk (Björk Guðmundsdóttir).
